Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a routine. I'd try to sit down and study, but my mind would wander and I'd end up just staring at my notes without absorbing anything. Some weeks I'd put in a lot of hours, like maybe 23 or so, but it felt scattered and unfocused. I learn better when I'm doing something hands-on, so just reading or watching videos made it hard to stay engaged. Most of the time I was just going through the motions.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place, to be honest. I'd tell myself I'd study at certain times, but then anxiety would hit and I'd avoid it completely. When deadlines got close, I'd panic and try to cram everything in at once. It wasn't healthy, but I didn't know how else to handle it. The regularity just never stuck for me.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline looming or feeling guilty about not doing anything for days. Sometimes I'd get a notification about something due and that would push me to log in. Other times I'd just check to see if I missed anything important, but I wouldn't stay long. It wasn't like I wanted to be there—it felt more like an obligation I was dreading.
Q4: Not really, no. I tried to make it a habit early on, but it never felt natural to me. The LMS always felt cold and impersonal, like I was just clicking through boxes. Without internet at home, I had to use campus computers, which made it harder to build any kind of consistent routine. It just never became automatic for me.
Q5: The videos were probably the most useful when I actually watched them. I could pause and rewind when I zoned out, which happened a lot. Discussion posts helped sometimes because seeing other students' perspectives made me feel less alone in struggling. But honestly, nothing felt like it was designed for someone like me who needs to physically do things to understand them.
Q6: I ignored a lot of the reading materials and supplementary resources. They just felt overwhelming when I was already struggling to keep up with the basics. I'd tell myself I'd get to them later, but later never came. The discussion boards I barely touched because putting my thoughts out there made my anxiety spike. I was scared of saying something stupid.
Q7: If I could see how it directly connected to passing the course or an assignment, I'd make myself do it. Activities that felt abstract or optional just didn't register as important when I was already drowning. I needed to feel like my effort would actually lead somewhere concrete. Otherwise, my brain would convince me it wasn't worth the stress.
Q8: Required activities felt like weights I had to carry, while optional ones felt like extra weights I could drop. When you're already struggling to stay afloat, anything labeled 'optional' gets cut immediately. I know that probably hurt my learning, but I just didn't have the mental energy to take on more than the bare minimum most days.
Q9: Yeah, I did a lot of studying with downloaded stuff and my own notes. Since I don't have internet at home, I'd download what I could when I was on campus and then work through it later in my room. I'd write things out by hand because that helps me remember better than just reading on a screen. A lot of my actual learning happened away from any computer.
Q10: There were plenty of times I was studying from printed notes or working through problems on paper at home. None of that would show up anywhere online. I'd spend hours trying to understand a concept by drawing diagrams or explaining it out loud to myself. The LMS would just show I hadn't logged in, but I was actually putting in work.
Q11: Definitely. I'd watch a video once on campus, take notes, and then study from those notes at home for days. Or I'd download a PDF and mark it up with a pen. The LMS would only show that one access, but I was returning to that material over and over in physical form. It's just how I have to work given my situation.
Q12: There were weeks where everything felt impossible. My anxiety would get so bad that even thinking about coursework made my chest tight. When I felt sad, which was a lot, I'd just shut down and avoid everything. The course material kept piling up while I was paralyzed, which made the anxiety worse. It was a vicious cycle I couldn't break out of.
Q13: All of those things hit me hard. I'd be exhausted from not sleeping well because of stress, then I'd log into the LMS and feel overwhelmed by everything I'd fallen behind on. Having limited resources meant I couldn't always access help when I needed it. Some weeks I had other responsibilities that ate into my study time, and the LMS work would just get pushed aside.
Q14: Oh, for sure. I'd open videos and let them play while my mind was somewhere else entirely. I'd scroll through discussion posts without really reading them. Sometimes I'd have the LMS open for an hour but couldn't tell you a single thing I learned. My body was there but my brain had checked out because I was too anxious or sad to focus.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even when I knew the deadline was coming. The anxiety made starting feel impossible—like there was this wall between me and the work. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, and then tomorrow I'd feel even worse about having waited. It wasn't laziness; it was like being frozen and watching yourself fail in slow motion.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to do anything. I'd ignore the LMS for days, then panic-log in the night before something was due. It wasn't a good system, but the pressure was the only thing that could cut through my avoidance. Most of my LMS activity was probably clustered right before due dates.
Q17: Feedback was complicated for me. When I got positive feedback, it helped a little, but my brain would dismiss it. Negative feedback made me want to disappear and never look at the course again. I'd avoid checking grades because I was scared of what I'd see. The CBT sessions helped me recognize these patterns, but changing them was still hard.
Q18: The structure felt rigid in ways that didn't work for how I learn. Everything was laid out in a linear way, but my brain doesn't work linearly, especially when I'm anxious. I needed more flexibility and hands-on options. The weekly modules felt like checkboxes I was failing to tick rather than actual learning opportunities.
Q19: Having everything online made it harder since I don't have internet at home. The lack of interactive or hands-on activities was tough for someone like me who learns by doing. When there were too many resources in one module, I'd freeze up and not know where to start. Simpler, more tactile options would have helped me a lot.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me—logging in, clicking around, trying to engage but not being able to actually produce anything. When your anxiety is high, you might spend hours in the LMS without accomplishing anything because you're stuck in avoidance mode. The activity looks like engagement but it's really just panicked scrolling.
Q21: That kind of describes me in a way. I passed the course despite my low LMS activity because I was doing a lot of studying offline that didn't get tracked. My exam score was decent because I'd actually learned the material through my own methods. The LMS numbers make me look worse than I actually was as a learner.
Q22: A course log can show when you clicked on things and how long you had them open, but it can't show what was going on in your head. It misses all the offline studying, the emotional struggles, the times you were engaged but anxious, or present but dissociating. It's like measuring someone's fitness by counting how many times they walked into a gym.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and feeling connected to it somehow. It's not just showing up—it's being mentally present and wanting to understand. For me, real engagement happened in quiet moments with my notes, not clicking through an LMS. It's about the quality of attention, not the quantity of logins.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I was struggling emotionally or how hard I was trying despite that. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent studying from printed notes at home or the anxiety attacks before logging in. It definitely wouldn't show that the CBT sessions actually helped me feel better about myself, even if my task completion stayed low. There's a whole person behind those numbers that the data can't see.
